items.title: DiGraphExport

milestone.title: cluster: di-graph
milestone.description: Cluster tracked in roadmap/clusters/di-graph/. Parent: see cluster README for scope and per-concept files.

items.body:
**Cluster:** di-graph · **Status:** planned · **Severity:** n/a (tool mode)

## Catches

N/A — this is a `krit di-graph` subcommand rather than a rule. Emits
a DOT / JSON / mermaid representation of the project's DI graph
for documentation or review.

## Shape

```
$ krit di-graph :app --format mermaid > app-graph.md
$ krit di-graph :app --format json | jq '.components[0].bindings | length'
$ krit di-graph --format dot > di-graph.dot
```

Proposed CLI shape:

- Parse the subcommand in `cmd/krit/main.go` alongside the existing verb handling for `baseline-audit`.
- Accept one optional positional module selector (`:app`, `:feature:data`, etc.). Reuse `internal/module.ModuleGraph`'s `Modules` map and `FileToModule()` to filter bindings to the requested Gradle module.
- Accept `--format=json|dot|mermaid`, with JSON as the easiest first ship because the graph already has stable structs in `internal/di/graph.go`: `Graph`, `Binding`, and `Dependency`.
- Emit bindings keyed by fully-qualified name, including `modulePath`, `scope`, `file`, `line`, and outbound dependency edges. DOT / mermaid can be thin formatters over the same in-memory payload.

## Dispatch

The cheapest implementation path is:

1. Discover modules with `module.DiscoverModules(scanRoot)` in `internal/module/discover.go`.
2. Populate module-to-module edges with `module.ParseAllDependencies(graph)` in `internal/module/depparse.go` so exported JSON can include both binding edges and Gradle-module context.
3. Collect Kotlin sources with `scanner.CollectKotlinFiles(paths, nil)` and parse them with `scanner.ScanFiles(paths, workers)` from `internal/scanner/scanner.go`.
4. Build the DI graph with `di.BuildGraph(files, moduleGraph)` in `internal/di/graph.go`.
5. Filter the resulting `graph.Bindings` by `Binding.ModulePath` when the user passes a module argument, then hand that filtered graph to a formatter layer.

This should not invent a separate walker. The same `di.BuildGraph` output is the natural substrate for this export subcommand, `whole-graph-binding-completeness`, `di-cycle-detection`, and `dead-bindings`.

## Infra Reuse

- The existing module-aware analysis setup in `cmd/krit/main.go` already shows the right ordering: `module.DiscoverModules(...)`, then `module.ParseAllDependencies(...)`, then downstream analysis.
- `internal/di/graph.go` already normalizes constructor-injected bindings, resolves imports/package-local references via `resolveType(...)`, records source locations, and tags each binding with `ModulePath`.
- `internal/di/scope.go` already gives exportable lifecycle metadata through `ResolveScope(...)` and `Scope`.
- `internal/di/graph_test.go` is the closest existing fixture for expected graph shape across multiple Gradle modules; future subcommand tests should reuse that mini-project pattern.
- Formatter code should be new, but isolated: for example `internal/di/export_json.go`, `internal/di/export_dot.go`, and `internal/di/export_mermaid.go`, each consuming `*di.Graph` without re-parsing files.

## Links

- Parent: `../README.md`

items.title: Move Dagre layout to Web Worker for large graph performance
items.body:
## References
User-reported: carrot-app.json (1192 components, 1285 links, 129 external links) freezes browser on full graph view

## Summary
The Dagre layout computation in computeDagreLayout.ts runs synchronously on the main thread. For graphs with ~1300 nodes and ~1400 edges, this blocks the browser for several seconds. Additionally, the mouseenter handler in ForceGraph.tsx filters all links O(n) on every hover. Solution: move Dagre to a Web Worker, add loading state, and precompute hover connection maps.

## Full Details
1. Create dagre-layout.worker.ts Web Worker that receives serializable node/edge data and returns positions. 2. Create useDagreLayout hook to manage the worker lifecycle and expose positions + computing state. 3. Update ForceGraph.tsx to use async layout with a loading indicator while computing. 4. Precompute a Map of node connection counts from links so hover lookups are O(1) instead of O(n). Key files: apps/eclair/src/platform/infra/graph/ForceGraph/computeDagreLayout.ts, ForceGraph.tsx, graph-types.ts

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dagre layout runs in a Web Worker off the main thread
- [ ] Loading indicator shown while layout computes
- [ ] Hover tooltip uses precomputed connection counts (O(1) instead of O(n))
- [ ] carrot-app.json loads without freezing the browser
- [ ] All existing tests pass
- [ ] 100% test coverage maintained
- [ ] A mergeable PR is ready for user review, created via /complete-task

items.title: Add RTFM — open-source multi-domain retrieval layer for AI agents
items.body:
Hi! I'd like to suggest adding **RTFM** to the awesome-mcp list.

## RTFM — Retrieve The Forgotten Memory

An open-source retrieval layer (MCP server) for AI agents. Indexes codebases, docs, and knowledge bases using SQLite + FTS5 — runs locally, no cloud, no API keys.

### Key features

- **10 parsers**: Markdown, Python (AST), LaTeX, PDF, YAML, JSON, Shell, XML, HTML, plaintext
- **FTS5 + semantic + hybrid search** via 13 MCP tools
- **Knowledge graph**: resolves `[[wikilinks]]` and Python imports as graph edges, hub detection, centrality ranking
- **Obsidian vault integration**: `rtfm vault` generates navigable `_rtfm/` files
- **Progressive disclosure**: metadata first (~300 tokens), then expand only what's needed
- **MCP native**: works with Claude Code, Cursor, Codex — any MCP client

### Benchmark results (FeatureBench, ICLR 2026)

- On mlflow (8,260 files): resolve rate went from 55-64% → 100% with RTFM
- Token consumption: -61%, cost: -51% on real documentation tasks

### Links

- **GitHub**: https://github.com/roomi-fields/rtfm
- **PyPI**: https://pypi.org/project/rtfm-ai/
- **MCP Registry**: `io.github.roomi-fields/rtfm`
- **License**: MIT

### Suggested category

Knowledge & Memory, or Search

Thanks!

items.title: [Server Submission]: Genesys Memory — Causal Graph Memory Engine for AI Agents
items.body:
### GitHub Repository URL

https://github.com/rishimeka/genesys

### Logo Image

No logo available at this time. Will update this issue when a 400x400 PNG is ready.

### Installation Testing

- [x] I have tested that Cline can successfully set up this server using only the README.md and/or llms-install.md file
- [x] The server is stable and ready for public use

### Additional Information

**Genesys Memory** is an open-source causal graph memory engine for AI agents.

- **PyPI:** [`genesys-memory`](https://pypi.org/project/genesys-memory/)
- **License:** AGPLv3
- **Install:** `pip install genesys-memory`
- **Run MCP server:** `python -m genesys_memory` (stdio transport)

**What it does:**

Every memory is a node in a unified causal graph. Edges represent causal relationships, and memories are scored using a multiplicative formula: `relevance × connectivity × reactivation`. The engine implements ACT-R base-level activation, spreading activation across graph edges, Rescorla-Wagner edge strengthening, and conjunctive active forgetting (a memory is only pruned when it is simultaneously irrelevant, causally orphaned, and not pinned).

**11 MCP tools exposed:**

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `memory_store` | Store a memory with automatic causal edge inference |
| `memory_recall` | Retrieve memories via vector search + graph traversal + spreading activation |
| `memory_search` | Keyword and semantic search |
| `memory_traverse` | Walk the causal graph from a given node |
| `memory_explain` | Explain why a memory was scored/ranked the way it was |
| `pin_memory` | Pin a memory to prevent forgetting |
| `unpin_memory` | Unpin a previously pinned memory |
| `list_core_memories` | List all core (long-term) memories |
| `delete_memory` | Manually delete a memory |
| `memory_stats` | Get statistics about the memory graph |
| `set_core_preferences` | Set user preferences for core memory promotion |

**Why Cline users will want this:**

Cline agents lose context between sessions. Genesys Memory gives them persistent, intelligent memory that strengthens through use — memories accessed together form stronger causal links (spreading activation), frequently recalled memories gain stability toward permanent core status, and irrelevant memories naturally decay. This means Cline can remember your project architecture, past debugging sessions, user preferences, and decision rationale across conversations without unbounded context growth.

**Setup for Cline:**

Add to your `cline_mcp_settings.json`:
```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "genesys-memory": {
      "command": "python",
      "args": ["-m", "genesys_memory"],
      "env": {}
    }
  }
}
```

No API keys required for base functionality. Optional extras for embeddings:
- `pip install "genesys-memory[local]"` — local sentence-transformers (no API key)
- `pip install "genesys-memory[openai]"` — OpenAI embeddings (requires `OPENAI_API_KEY`)
- `pip install "genesys-memory[anthropic]"` — LLM-based memory processing

items.title: GC-E012: Sensitive Artifact Exclusion Boundary
items.body:
> **GC-E012** | CONSTRAINT | SHOULD | Wave 3 | DRAFT

## Statement

The system shall support configurable exclusion patterns that prevent sensitive artifacts (secrets, credentials, API keys, private key files, `.env` files, and other configurable patterns) from being linked as traced entities in the traceability graph. Exclusion patterns shall apply to: manual artifact linking (GC-E001), automated UID scanning (GC-E010), test UID scanning (GC-E006), and evidence collection imports. Attempted links to excluded artifacts shall be rejected with a warning identifying the exclusion rule that matched. The default exclusion set shall include common secret file patterns (`.env`, `*credentials*`, `*secret*`, `*.pem`, `*.key`) and shall be overridable per project.

## Rationale

The traceability graph stores artifact identifiers, URLs, and metadata. If a `.env` file or `credentials.json` is linked as a CONFIG artifact, the artifact identifier is visible to all graph consumers, and evidence collection (GC-I003, GC-S001) could pull the file's contents into the evidence store. No existing requirement guards this boundary. This is a security concern that becomes critical in multi-tenant (GC-P006) and agentic (GC-O004) scenarios where agents create links autonomously.

---
*Created from Ground Control requirement GC-E012*

items.title: [Feature] Graph-driven wiki maintenance: feed structural insights back into wiki ops
items.body:
## Problem

The knowledge graph (`tools/build_graph.py`) currently operates as a **read-only visualization endpoint**:

```
wiki pages → build_graph.py → graph.json + graph.html → user looks at the graph → end
```

The graph computes valuable structural insights — community clusters, hub nodes, orphan nodes, bridge edges — but **none of this information flows back** into wiki maintenance decisions. The graph and the wiki are two separate artifacts that never talk to each other.

### Evidence from a 620-node graph

After building the graph on a real wiki with 620 pages:

| Metric | Value | What it means |
|---|---|---|
| Total nodes | 620 | — |
| Total edges | 394 | Sparse — only 0.64 edges/node |
| **Orphan nodes** | **382 (61.6%)** | Pages with zero connections to anything |
| Communities detected | 404 | Extreme fragmentation — almost 1 community per node |
| Largest community | 39 nodes | Only 6.3% of the wiki is in the biggest cluster |
| Hub node (max degree) | `entities/PersonA` — 57 connections | 14.5% of all edges touch a single node |
| Leaf nodes | 83 | Pages connected by exactly 1 edge |
| Single-bridge community pairs | 8 | Communities connected by only 1 edge — fragile |

These numbers tell a clear story: **the wiki is a massive orphan halo around a few hub nodes**, and the graph knows this but can't do anything about it.

### What the graph knows that the wiki doesn't

| Graph insight | Actionable wiki operation | Currently happens? |
|---|---|---|
| Node has 0 connections | Page is likely missing `[[wikilinks]]` — re-scan and add links | ❌ No |
| Two concepts always co-occur in the same community | Suggest merging into one page or adding cross-references | ❌ No |
| Node is a hub (top 5 by degree centrality) | Its page should be comprehensive — flag if it's a stub | ❌ No |
| Two communities connected by single edge | Create a synthesis page bridging the gap | ❌ No |
| Isolated subgraph (no path to main component) | Likely a naming mismatch or missing source — flag for user review | ❌ No |

## Proposed solution: `--report` flag on build_graph.py

### Phase 1: Graph health report (no wiki mutation)

Add a `--report` flag to `build_graph.py` that outputs a structured **Graph Insights Report**:

```bash
python tools/build_graph.py --report
```

Output:

```markdown
# Graph Insights Report — 2026-04-14

## Health Summary
- 620 nodes, 394 edges (0.64 edges/node — below healthy threshold of 2.0)
- 382 orphan nodes (61.6%) — target: <10%
- 404 communities — extreme fragmentation

## Recommended Actions

### 🔴 Orphan Remediation (382 pages)
These pages have zero connections. They likely need wikilinks added:
- wiki/concepts/API Key Isolation.md
- wiki/concepts/Active Period Sampling Rate.md
- ... (full list)

### 🟡 Hub Pages Need Depth Check (5 pages)
These hub nodes carry disproportionate connectivity. Verify they're comprehensive:
- entities/PersonA.md — 57 connections (14.5% of all edges)
- entities/ProjectX.md — 16 connections
- ...

### 🟡 Fragile Bridges (8 community pairs)
These community pairs are connected by only 1 edge — one deleted link breaks them apart:
- Community 12 ↔ Community 45 via entities/RAG.md
- ...

### 🟢 Merge Candidates
Concepts always appearing together in the same community:
- "Desensitization (脱敏)" + "Desensitized Assets" → consider merging
- ...
```

This report can be:
- Printed to stdout (default)
- Saved to `wiki/graph-report.md` with `--save`
- Used as input for a future `compact` workflow (#23)

### Phase 2: Graph-guided lint (future)

Integrate graph insights into the existing `lint` workflow:
- `lint` already checks for orphan pages and broken links
- Add graph-derived checks: hub stubs, fragile bridges, merge candidates
- This creates a feedback loop: `ingest → graph → lint → fix → ingest`

### Phase 3: Graph-guided auto-linking (future, needs design discussion)

The most impactful but riskiest step: automatically add `[[wikilinks]]` to pages based on graph analysis. This would require:
- Confidence thresholds (only link when similarity > X)
- Dry-run mode (show proposed links without applying)
- User approval for batch operations

## Architecture diagram

```
Current (one-way):
  wiki/ ──→ build_graph.py ──→ graph.json + graph.html

Proposed (feedback loop):
  wiki/ ──→ build_graph.py ──→ graph.json + graph.html
                │
                ├──→ graph-report.md  (Phase 1: --report)
                │
                └──→ lint.py          (Phase 2: graph-aware lint)
                       │
                       └──→ wiki/     (Phase 3: auto-linking)
```

## Related

- #23 — Wiki entropy RFC (graph insights would directly feed merge/compaction decisions)
- #24 — Post-ingest validation (validation could expand to include graph checks)
- PR #21 — Q2 architecture refresh (adds Louvain communities and improved graph UI)

## Discussion questions

1. Should the graph report be a separate file (`graph-report.md`) or folded into the existing `lint-report.md`?
2. For Phase 3 auto-linking, what confidence threshold would be appropriate? Should it be configurable?
3. Is there interest in a `--watch` mode that rebuilds the graph and checks for new orphans after each ingest?

items.title: addLinksBatch: unnest(::text[]) silently drops rows when content contains array-syntax characters
items.body:
## Bug

`addLinksBatch` in both `PostgresEngine` and `PGLiteEngine` uses `unnest(::text[])` to batch-insert links. When any slug or context string contains `{`, `}`, `"`, or `,`, PostgreSQL interprets these as text[] array delimiters, producing a malformed array literal. The `unnest()` then yields an empty set and the entire batch INSERT returns zero rows — **with no error raised**.

## Impact

This is a silent data loss bug. In a CJK-heavy vault (~500 pages), approximately 60% of link rows were silently dropped during `gbrain extract links`. The link graph becomes incomplete, degrading search, backlinks, and brain-score accuracy.

Characters that trigger this:
- `{}` — extremely common in JSON snippets, code blocks, template syntax
- `"` — ubiquitous in quoted text, frontmatter, markdown
- `,` — present in nearly all natural language

Any vault with non-ASCII content or code blocks is affected.

## Reproduction

1. Create two pages with slugs containing no special characters
2. Add a link between them with a `context` field containing `{"key": "value"}`
3. Run `gbrain extract links`
4. Observe: the link is not inserted, but no error is logged

## Root Cause

`unnest($1::text[], $2::text[], ...)` requires each parameter to be a valid PostgreSQL text array literal. The postgres-js driver (and PGLite's query interface) serialize JavaScript arrays into the `{elem1,elem2,...}` format. When an element itself contains `{`, `}`, `"`, or `,`, the serialized literal becomes ambiguous and PostgreSQL silently misparses it.

## Suggested Fix

Replace `unnest(::text[])` with individual parameterized INSERTs using scalar `$N` parameters. Scalar parameters are immune to array-syntax parsing. Each link is inserted independently; a missing page only skips that single row.

Tradeoff: more round-trips per batch (N queries instead of 1), but typical batch sizes (50-200 links) complete in <100ms on local PostgreSQL.

items.title: Inline GO-CAM visualization using web-components
items.body:
## Summary

Render GO-CAM models directly in the curation interface using the `go-gocam-viewer` web component from [geneontology/web-components](https://github.com/geneontology/web-components). This lets curators see models as they're being built by the noctua agent, without needing to follow external links.

## Design

### Data flow (no external API, no new backend endpoint)

The `go-gocam-viewer` component has a `setModelData(json)` method that accepts model data directly — no API call needed. The Minerva/Barista response format (BBOP graph) is the same format the component expects.

The noctua tool's `get_model` action already returns the full BBOP graph JSON in its response. The data flows through the existing chat tool response path:

1. Noctua agent creates/modifies a model via noctua-py
2. Agent calls `get_model` — tool returns full BBOP graph JSON inline
3. Frontend detects model data in the chat tool response
4. Frontend passes it to `go-gocam-viewer.setModelData()`

No new backend endpoint needed — the model data is already flowing through the chat.

### Frontend integration

- Add `@geneontology/web-components` to the frontend
- Create a GO-CAM viewer panel (similar to the existing PDF viewer panel)
- Panel appears when the noctua agent returns model data in a chat response
- Panel refreshes as the agent adds individuals/facts and calls get_model again
- Display note: "Model is unsaved — save in Noctua to make it permanent"

### Key technical details

- Component: `<go-gocam-viewer>` with `setModelData()` method
- Data format: BBOP graph JSON (same as Minerva/Barista `get_model` response)
- No dependency on `api.geneontology.org` — all data comes through barista-dev via noctua-py
- No new backend endpoint — data flows inline through the chat tool response
- Component renders using Cytoscape.js with dagre layout
- Includes sidebar with activities grouped by biological process

## References

- [geneontology/web-components](https://github.com/geneontology/web-components) — source of `go-gocam-viewer`
- Component docs: https://geneontology.github.io/web-components/
- `setModelData()` method: accepts BBOP graph JSON directly, no fetch

## Charter item

> enable go-cam visualizations in the workflow

items.title: [Dreaming] Add JSON-LD structured data for personal brand SEO

labels.name: dreaming
labels.description: Ideas generated by daily dreaming agent

items.body:
## What
Add JSON-LD schema markup to \`index.html\` — specifically a \`Person\` schema with name, job title, social links, and a \`WebSite\` schema. This enables Google to display a rich knowledge panel and structured search results when someone searches for "Lai Kah Keen" or related terms.

## Why
The site already has solid Open Graph and Twitter card meta tags, but lacks machine-readable structured data. For a personal brand site meant to be "the single link I send people", showing up well in search results is high leverage. This is a one-time, low-maintenance addition that compounds over time as search indexers crawl the site.

## Effort
Small

## Key Files
- \`index.html\` (add \`<script type="application/ld+json">\` block in \`<head>\`)

## Goal Alignment
Serves GOALS.md direction: "Make this the single link I send people to understand what I do." Better discoverability means people can find the site without being directly sent the link.

items.title: feat: Project portability — UUID fields, template cloning, and cross-environment export/import
items.body:
## Summary

Add project portability features to enable two primary use cases:

1. **Template project cloning** — Copy a project's structure (deployments, sites, devices, pipeline configs, taxa, filters) into a new project on the same instance, without copying processed data (images, occurrences, detections)
2. **Cross-environment export/import** — Serialize a complete project graph to JSON and import it into a different Antenna instance (e.g., self-hosted → managed, prod → staging)

## Background

The `move_project_data` management command (#1192) handles moving deployments between projects on the same instance. This issue covers the complementary operations: cloning and cross-environment portability.

A detailed design spec exists on the `move-project-data` branch: `docs/claude/planning/project-portability-spec.md`

## Use Case 1: Template Project Cloning

Copy a project's configuration to create a new project with the same setup but no data.

**What gets copied:**
- Project settings (default filters, score thresholds, default pipeline)
- Deployment definitions (name, coordinates, S3 sources)
- Devices and Sites
- Pipeline configs and ProcessingService links
- Taxa lists and taxa M2M links
- Member list and roles
- Tags

**What does NOT get copied:**
- Source images, events, occurrences, detections, classifications, identifications
- Jobs
- Data exports

**Proposed interface:**
```bash
python manage.py clone_project --source-project 23 --name "New Project from Template"
```

## Use Case 2: Cross-Environment Export/Import

Serialize a full project (including all data) to a JSON interchange format and import into another Antenna instance.

**Prerequisites (from the portability spec):**
- UUID fields on all project-scoped models (18+ models)
- Natural key methods for shared references (Taxon, User, Pipeline, Algorithm)
- FK serialization using UUIDs for project-scoped and natural keys for global references

**Key design decisions documented in the spec:**
- Integer PKs remain internal; UUIDs are external identity
- Conflict handling modes: error, skip, update
- Export ordering follows FK dependency graph
- Source image files are NOT exported (S3 paths preserved; target instance needs access to same or copied bucket)

**Proposed interface:**
```bash
# Export
python manage.py export_project --project 23 --output nunavik-export.json

# Import
python manage.py import_project nunavik-export.json --on-conflict=skip --dry-run
```

## Implementation phases

### Phase 1: Template cloning (simpler, no UUIDs needed)
- [ ] `clone_project` management command
- [ ] Tests

### Phase 2: UUID infrastructure
- [ ] Add UUID fields to all project-scoped models (backfill migration)
- [ ] Natural key methods on models
- [ ] Add missing unique constraints (Deployment, Device, Site names within project)

### Phase 3: Export/Import
- [ ] `export_project` management command (JSON serialization with UUID-based FKs)
- [ ] `import_project` management command (conflict resolution, FK remapping)
- [ ] Tests

## Open research areas

Documented in the spec (`docs/claude/planning/project-portability-spec.md`):
- UUID backfill migration performance on large tables (SourceImage ~10M rows, Detection ~1M)
- Detection/Classification uniqueness constraints
- Taxonomy divergence between instances
- Darwin Core / GBIF identifier conventions
- Schema evolution between Antenna versions
